title: Autoboxing and Unboxing
description: Wrapper classes and Java's automatic conversion between primitives and their object equivalents -- finally explaining why ArrayList<Integer> could still hold numbers.add(5).
objectives: Understand wrapper classes as object equivalents of primitives
Understand autoboxing (primitive to wrapper) and unboxing (wrapper to primitive)
Recognize a common autoboxing pitfall with == comparisons

sections.text: In Array vs ArrayList and Generics, we said generics require reference types, so ArrayList<Integer> is used instead of ArrayList<int>. But then how did numbers.add(10); (passing a plain int literal) ever work? This lesson explains exactly that.
A reference type that represents a primitive value as an object, allowing it to be used wherever an object is required, such as in generic collections.

sections.title: Every primitive has a wrapper class

sections.code:
int primitiveNum = 10;
Integer wrappedNum = primitiveNum; // autoboxing -- happens automatically

sections.text: Java's automatic conversion of a primitive value into its corresponding wrapper object, whenever a wrapper (or reference type) is expected but a primitive is given.
Java's automatic conversion of a wrapper object back into its underlying primitive value, whenever a primitive is expected but a wrapper object is given.

This is exactly what was happening, silently, back in Array vs ArrayList. numbers.add(10); works because Java automatically boxes the int literal 10 into an Integer object before storing it, and numbers.get(0) automatically unboxes the stored Integer back into a usable int.
5. A Common Pitfall: == With Wrapper Objects

This surprising inconsistency happens because Java caches (reuses) Integer objects for small values, roughly -128 to 127, but creates brand NEW objects for values outside that range. Recall from the Immutability lesson: == compares object references, not content. a and b (both 100) happen to share a cached object, but c and d (both 200) don't. The fix is the same as for String: always compare wrapper object VALUES with .equals(), not ==.

for (int score : scores) works even though scores holds Integer objects -- each Integer is automatically unboxed into an int for the score variable on every iteration of this enhanced for loop (Control Flow).

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Comparing wrapper objects with == expecting it to always behave like comparing primitives -- it only appears to work for small cached values (roughly -128 to 127); always use .equals() instead.
Forgetting a wrapper object can be null, unlike a primitive -- unboxing a null Integer throws a NullPointerException, a risk that never exists with a plain int.
Assuming boxing/unboxing has zero cost -- each conversion creates or unwraps an object, which matters in performance-sensitive loops with heavy boxing.
Being surprised that ArrayList<Integer> 'just works' with int literals -- it's autoboxing happening silently, not the collection secretly supporting primitives directly.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is autoboxing?
questions.answer: Java's automatic conversion of a primitive value into its corresponding wrapper object, which happens whenever a reference type is expected but a primitive value is provided -- like passing an int literal into an ArrayList<Integer>.

questions.question: What is unboxing?
questions.answer: Java's automatic conversion of a wrapper object back into its underlying primitive value, happening whenever a primitive is expected but a wrapper object is given -- like assigning an Integer to an int variable.

questions.question: Why can Integer a = 100; Integer b = 100; a == b; return true, while Integer c = 200; Integer d = 200; c == d; returns false?
questions.answer: Java caches Integer objects for small values (roughly -128 to 127), so a and b happen to reference the same cached object. Values outside that range, like 200, create separate objects each time, so c == d compares two different object references and returns false. .equals() should always be used instead for reliable value comparison.

questions.question: Why can unboxing a null Integer cause a problem that a plain int never has?
questions.answer: A wrapper object can hold null, since it's a reference type, but a primitive cannot. Unboxing a null wrapper (assigning it to a primitive variable) throws a NullPointerException at runtime.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Autoboxing is Java's automatic conversion of a primitive into its wrapper object (like int into Integer), and unboxing is the reverse -- both happen silently wherever needed, which is exactly why ArrayList<Integer> could accept plain int literals via .add() despite generics requiring reference types. A notorious pitfall: Integer == comparisons only appear reliable for small cached values (roughly -128 to 127); outside that range, == compares different object references and can unexpectedly return false, so .equals() should always be used for wrapper value comparisons. Wrapper objects can also be null, unlike primitives, so unboxing a null wrapper throws a NullPointerException.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Autobox = primitive puts on an object COSTUME automatically. Unbox = the costume comes off automatically. Small Integers (-128 to 127) share a costume from a shared closet -- == can lie outside that range. .equals() never lies.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What is autoboxing? What is unboxing?
Why does Integer == comparison sometimes give surprising results?
What should you use instead of == to compare wrapper object values?
Why can unboxing throw a NullPointerException?
